word_list.word: (each|every) bird loves to hear himself sing
word_list.pronunciation: ˈiːtʃ ˈɛvɹi bˈɜːd lˈʌvz tə hˈɪɹ hɪmsˈɛlf sˈɪŋ

senses.definition: used to imply that people may have a tendency to talk too much about themselves or their own ideas and abilities, without considering the interests or perspectives of others

word_list.word: every potter praises his own pot
word_list.pronunciation: ˈɛvɹi pˈɑːɾɚ pɹˈeɪzᵻz hɪz ˈoʊn pˈɑːt

senses.definition: used to imply that people tend to have a high opinion of their own abilities or accomplishments, and may not always be objective or accurate in their self-assessment
used to suggest that someone who makes a lot of noise or boasts excessively may not be as capable or effective as they claim to be

word_list.pronunciation: duːnˌɑːt bˈɑːɹk ɪf juː kænˈɑːt bˈaɪt

senses.definition: used to advise that it is better to avoid making empty threats or promises, as it can result in loss of respect or credibility
used to suggest that those who have experienced threats or danger may be better equipped to handle potential risks or dangers in the future due to their past experiences

examples.x: After the break-in, Dan installed new security systems and upgraded all the locks because he believed threatened men live long and he wanted to be prepared if it happened again.

word_list.word: in a calm sea every man is a pilot
word_list.pronunciation: ɪn ɐ kˈɑːm sˈiː ˈɛvɹi mˈæn ɪz ɐ pˈaɪlət

senses.definition: used to suggest that people often overestimate their abilities or expertise when they are not being challenged, and that true skill and leadership are only tested in difficult or challenging situations

examples.x: As a young lawyer, I quickly learned that in a calm sea every man is a pilot when I struggled to handle a difficult case that required more expertise than I had initially thought.
